content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 1, 1991


Steven L. Phillips, M.D.
Chief of Medicine
Veterans Home of California
P.O. Box 1200
Yountville, CA 94599-1297

Re:  Your Request For Confirmation
of Telephone Advice
Our File No. A-91-137

Dear Dr. Phillips:

You have requested confirmation of telephone advice I gave
you on February 25, 1991, concerning the honorarium prohibitions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Is money you receive for lectures related to your field of
practice and research prohibited honoraria under the Act?

CONCLUSION

The money you receive for lectures related to your field of
specialty, practice and research are not prohibited honoraria
under the Act.

FACTS

You are currently chief of medicine at the Veterans Home of
California in Yountville.  You give lectures to health care
professionals including physicians, nurses and pharmacists.  The


_______________________
1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-91-137
·Page 2


lectures are related to the areas of medicine that you specialize,
practice, and do research in. The fees for the lectures are paid
by pharmaceutical companies.

ANALYSIS

Under Section 89503(a):

[N]o designated employee of a state agency,
shall accept an honorium from any source, if
the . . . employee would be required to report
the receipt of income or gifts from that
source on his or her statement of economic
interest.

Because you are chief of medicine at the Veterans Home of
California, we assume that you are a designated employee of the
Department of Veterans Affairs requiring full disclosure of all
income. Under this assumption, all income you receive is to be
reported on your statement of economic interests. (Section
87302(b).)

The question is whether the money you receive for your
lectures constitutes an "honorarium." As defined by the Act,

"Honorarium" means . . . any payment made in
consideration for any speech given, article
published, or attendance at any public or
private conference, convention, meeting,
social event, meal, or like gathering.

Section 89502(b).

However "honorarium" does not include:

. . . earned income for personal services
which are customarily provided in connection
with the individual's business or practice of
a bona fide business, trade, or profession,
such as teaching, practicing law, medicine,
insurance, real estate, banking, or building
contracting, unless the sole or predominant
activity of the business, trade, or profession
is making speeches. . . .

Section 89502(c), emphasis added.

Medicine is a bona fide business, trade or profession
specifically exempted from the scope of the honoraria
prohibitions. (Section 89502(c).) Under the facts you have
presented, it appears that your lectures fall within this
exception. Your lectures are given to health care professionals
related to areas of medicine in which you practice and do
research. Under these circumstances the money you receive for

Our File No. A-91-137
Page 3


your lectures falls within the exception of Section 89502(c) and
is not a prohibited honorarium.2

If you have any further questions regarding this matter
please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Mark T. Morodomi
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:MM:dg




2 Please note, however, that the pharmaceutical companies that
pay you for your lectures are sources of income to you. As such,
you may have a conflict of interest if you make, participate in
making or otherwise use your official position to influence a
governmental decision that will foreseeably and materially affect
the pharmaceutical companies. (Section 87100, 87103.) For
example, you may have a conflict of interest when you participate
in hospital decisions concerning the pharmaceutical companies that
have made payments to you.

Your lectures falls within the exception of Section 89502(c) and is not a prohibited honorarium.2

QUESTION: Is money you receive for lectures related to your field of practice and research prohibited honoraria under the Act?

CONCLUSION: The money you receive for lectures related to your field of specialty, practice and research are not prohibited honoraria under the Act.
